threats.title: Broken Security.sol Modifiers Enable Complete Protocol Takeover and Fund Theft
threats.hypothesis_description: The onlyOwner and onlyManager modifiers in Security.sol evaluate boolean expressions (msg.sender == owner;) as no-op statements without require(), meaning they NEVER revert. Any external address can call all protected functions including transferOwnership, setManager, setProtocolFeePercent, setFeeRedistributor, setERC20Factory, setMaxFeePercent, setExternalFeePercent, addFees, and onBalanceChange.
threats.investigation_rationale: This is the most severe bug: an attacker can take full ownership, redirect all fee destinations to themselves, replace FeeSplitter and ERC20Factory with malicious contracts, and drain protocol value through every subsequent trade.

proof_of_signal.pattern: modifier onlyOwner() { msg.sender == owner; _; } — boolean expression evaluated as statement, never reverts

preconditions: Contracts are deployed (any state)
No external wrapper or proxy adding access control

initial_economic_evidence.suspicious_calculation: msg.sender == owner; evaluates to true/false but result is discarded
initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: Call transferOwnership(attacker) then setProtocolFeePercent(maxPercent, attacker) to redirect all protocol fees
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: All future trade fees routed to attacker; can also replace feeRedistributor to steal holder fees

economic_questions: What is the total TVL and daily fee volume at risk?
Can attacker set fees to 100% and drain all buy ETH?
validation_requirements: Confirm modifiers compile without require by deploying and calling from non-owner
Verify Solidity 0.8.7 treats standalone expression as valid statement

preliminary_attack_economics.potential_profit_range: All protocol fees + potential for draining all future trade value

market_conditions_analysis.optimal_timing: Immediately — before someone else exploits it

potential_victim_impact.direct_victims: All protocol users and the protocol itself
potential_victim_impact.total_value_at_risk: Entire protocol TVL and all accumulated fees

investigation_priorities.parameter_analysis: Verify modifier bytecode contains no REVERT opcode
investigation_priorities.calculation_validation: Test calling transferOwnership from non-owner account

threats.title: Unprotected setCurves() Enables Complete FeeSplitter Drain via Malicious Contract
threats.hypothesis_description: FeeSplitter.setCurves() has NO access control modifier whatsoever. An attacker can deploy a malicious contract that returns fabricated balance/supply values, call setCurves() to point to it, then call claimFees() to drain all ETH from the FeeSplitter. The malicious contract returns a huge balance for the attacker (inflating owed fees) and the attacker claims everything.
threats.investigation_rationale: This is a standalone critical vulnerability independent of the Security.sol modifier bug. Even if modifiers were fixed, setCurves() would still be callable by anyone.

proof_of_signal.pattern: function setCurves(Curves curves_) public { curves = curves_; } — no modifier, no access check

preconditions: FeeSplitter has ETH balance (fees have been deposited)
At least one token has non-zero cumulativeFeePerToken

initial_economic_evidence.suspicious_calculation: balanceOf reads from curves contract; attacker controls what curves returns
initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: Deploy contract returning huge curvesTokenBalance for attacker → inflated owed in getClaimableFees
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: Attacker claims entire FeeSplitter ETH balance in single transaction

economic_questions: What is the total ETH balance in FeeSplitter at any given time?
Can the attacker also manipulate addFees via the fake curves to maximize extraction?
validation_requirements: Deploy mock contract with inflated balances, call setCurves, then claimFees
Verify no other check prevents claim with fabricated balance

preliminary_attack_economics.estimated_capital_needed: Gas for deploying mock + 2 transactions (~0.01 ETH)

market_conditions_analysis.optimal_timing: When FeeSplitter has accumulated significant fees

potential_victim_impact.direct_victims: All token holders expecting fee distributions

investigation_priorities.parameter_analysis: Check if curves reference is validated anywhere after setCurves
investigation_priorities.calculation_validation: Trace claimFees with fabricated balance inputs

threats.title: FeeSplitter Insolvency via Transfer-Based Fee Double-Claiming
threats.hypothesis_description: The _transfer function does not notify FeeSplitter (no onBalanceChange call). When tokens are transferred to a fresh address, the recipient's userFeeOffset is 0, allowing them to claim all historical fees. If the sender claims first, then transfers to a fresh address which claims again, the FeeSplitter pays out more than it holds, becoming insolvent and leaving other holders unable to claim.
threats.investigation_rationale: This is a fundamental accounting flaw: the fee distribution system assumes offsets are synchronized with balance changes, but transfers bypass the synchronization entirely.

proof_of_signal.pattern: _transfer updates balances without calling feeRedistributor.onBalanceChange for sender or receiver

preconditions: Attacker holds curves tokens for a subject with accumulated holdersFees
cumulativeFeePerToken > 0 for the subject token
FeeSplitter has ETH balance

initial_economic_evidence.suspicious_calculation: Recipient offset = 0; owed = cumulativeFeePerToken * balance — claims ALL historical fees
initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: 1) Claim fees from address A. 2) Transfer tokens to fresh address B. 3) Claim from B (offset=0 → full historical fees). 4) Repeat with address C, D...
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: Each cycle extracts cumulativeFeePerToken * tokenBalance worth of ETH until FeeSplitter is empty

economic_questions: How large is cumulativeFeePerToken after sustained trading activity?
How many cycles before FeeSplitter is fully drained?
Does .transfer() 2300 gas limit prevent re-entrancy during the drain?
validation_requirements: Simulate: claim → transfer → claim cycle and verify double payment
Calculate total extractable vs FeeSplitter balance
Test with multiple subjects/tokens

preliminary_attack_economics.estimated_capital_needed: Cost to buy initial tokens on bonding curve + gas
preliminary_attack_economics.estimated_execution_cost: Gas for claim + transfer per cycle (~0.01 ETH per cycle)
preliminary_attack_economics.attack_duration: Multiple transactions, each cycle drains more

market_conditions_analysis.optimal_timing: After significant fee accumulation in FeeSplitter

potential_victim_impact.direct_victims: All token holders with unclaimed fees
potential_victim_impact.estimated_impact_per_user: 100% loss of claimable fees once FeeSplitter is insolvent

investigation_priorities.parameter_analysis: Typical cumulativeFeePerToken values and FeeSplitter balance
investigation_priorities.calculation_validation: Trace getClaimableFees for fresh address with offset=0

threats.title: Fee Manipulation via Broken Access Control Enables Sell Lockout and Fund Trapping
threats.hypothesis_description: Due to broken onlyManager modifier, anyone can call setMaxFeePercent followed by setExternalFeePercent to set total fees ≥ 100% (1 ether). When totalFee >= price, getSellPriceAfterFee underflows (price - totalFee), and _transferFees computes negative sellValue. Sellers cannot sell tokens, permanently trapping their funds in the bonding curve.
threats.investigation_rationale: This combines the access control vulnerability with fee economics to create an irreversible fund-trapping scenario. Even after the fees are fixed, users who bought at high prices during manipulation may suffer losses.

proof_of_signal.pattern: sellValue = price - protocolFee - subjectFee - referralFee - holderFee — underflows if total fees >= price

preconditions: Broken onlyManager modifier (confirmed in Security.sol)
maxFeePercent has no hard upper bound vs 1 ether

initial_economic_evidence.suspicious_calculation: setMaxFeePercent(2 ether) then setExternalFeePercent(0.5 ether, 0.3 ether, 0.3 ether) → fees = 110%+ of price
initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: Set fees > 100%, all sells revert with underflow, then extort users or wait
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: Attacker buys at normal fees, sets fees to >100%, nobody can sell, then resets fees and sells at profit as only seller

economic_questions: Can the attacker also set protocolFeePercent to redirect buy fees to themselves while sells are blocked?
Is there a Solidity 0.8.7 checked arithmetic revert in _transferFees for the underflow?
validation_requirements: Set fees above 100% and confirm sell reverts
Verify no other bounds check prevents this configuration

preliminary_attack_economics.potential_profit_range: All tokens become unsellable — total bonding curve TVL at risk
preliminary_attack_economics.estimated_profit_margin: Infinite if combined with fee destination redirection

potential_victim_impact.direct_victims: All token holders across all curves

investigation_priorities.calculation_validation: Confirm sell path reverts when totalFee > price

threats.title: Anyone Can Call onBalanceChange to Destroy Victims' Accumulated Fees
threats.hypothesis_description: Due to the broken onlyManager modifier, anyone can call FeeSplitter.onBalanceChange(token, victim). This function sets userFeeOffset[victim] = cumulativeFeePerToken WITHOUT first calling updateFeeCredit to preserve accrued fees. All of the victim's uncollected fees are permanently destroyed.
threats.investigation_rationale: This is a targeted griefing attack that can steal from specific users. The attacker doesn't profit directly but can cause victim losses, or combine with other exploits (e.g., transfer to fresh address to claim the freed fees).

proof_of_signal.pattern: onBalanceChange sets data.userFeeOffset[account] = data.cumulativeFeePerToken without calling updateFeeCredit first

preconditions: Victim has accumulated unclaimed fees (cumulativeFeePerToken > userFeeOffset)
Broken onlyManager modifier allows anyone to call onBalanceChange

initial_economic_evidence.suspicious_calculation: onBalanceChange: data.userFeeOffset[account] = data.cumulativeFeePerToken — skips owed = (cumFPT - offset) * balance calculation
initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: Call onBalanceChange(token, victim) for every victim with accumulated fees
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: Destroyed fees remain in FeeSplitter; attacker with tokens can claim a larger share of remaining pool

economic_questions: Can the attacker profit by destroying others' fees and claiming a proportionally larger share?
How much total value can be wiped across all users?
validation_requirements: Call onBalanceChange externally, verify victim's claimable drops to 0
Verify destroyed fees are not recoverable

preliminary_attack_economics.potential_profit_range: Indirect: proportional increase in attacker's fee share
preliminary_attack_economics.estimated_profit_margin: Depends on ratio of destroyed fees to total

market_conditions_analysis.optimal_timing: When victims have large unclaimed fee balances

potential_victim_impact.direct_victims: Any token holder with unclaimed fees
potential_victim_impact.total_value_at_risk: Total unclaimed fees across all users

investigation_priorities.parameter_analysis: Typical unclaimed fee accumulation per user

threats.title: Trading DoS via ERC20 Wrapping — All Buys and Sells Blocked
threats.hypothesis_description: FeeSplitter.totalSupply excludes tokens held by the Curves contract (ERC20-wrapped tokens). If all circulating tokens for a subject are wrapped via withdraw(), totalSupply becomes 0, causing addFees() to revert with NoTokenHolders. Since _transferFees calls addFees when holdersFeePercent > 0, all buyCurvesToken and sellCurvesToken transactions revert, completely halting trading for that subject.
threats.investigation_rationale: A subject who is the sole token holder can withdraw their single token and permanently block all trading for their curve. New buyers cannot enter because the buy transaction itself reverts.

proof_of_signal.pattern: totalSupply = (curvesTokenSupply - curvesTokenBalance[token][address(curves)]) * PRECISION — becomes 0 when all tokens wrapped

preconditions: holdersFeePercent > 0 and feeRedistributor != address(0)
Subject or dominant holder wraps all circulating tokens to ERC20

initial_economic_evidence.suspicious_calculation: totalSupply_ == 0 → addFees reverts → _transferFees reverts → buy/sell reverts
initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: Subject creates curve (supply=1), withdraws 1 token → totalSupply = 0 → all trading blocked
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: Griefing: lock users out of trading; or strategic: create artificial scarcity by controlling when trading is possible

economic_questions: Can a non-subject large holder perform this attack by wrapping all their tokens?
Is the subject forced to deposit back to unblock, giving them ransom leverage?
validation_requirements: Withdraw all circulating tokens, then attempt buyCurvesToken — confirm revert
Verify deposit restores trading

preliminary_attack_economics.estimated_capital_needed: Cost of initial token purchase + gas
preliminary_attack_economics.potential_profit_range: DoS — no direct profit but blocks all value flow

market_conditions_analysis.liquidity_requirements: Must control all circulating tokens (easiest for new curves)
market_conditions_analysis.optimal_timing: Immediately after curve creation (subject holds all tokens)
market_conditions_analysis.market_impact_assessment: Complete trading halt for affected curve

potential_victim_impact.direct_victims: All potential traders for the affected curve
potential_victim_impact.estimated_impact_per_user: Cannot buy or sell at all

investigation_priorities.parameter_analysis: Verify holdersFeePercent is typically > 0 in production

threats.title: Withdraw/Deposit Cycle Exploits Stale Fee Offsets for Fee Misappropriation
threats.hypothesis_description: Neither withdraw() nor deposit() calls FeeSplitter.onBalanceChange. When a user withdraws tokens (moving internal tokens to Curves contract), their FeeSplitter offset stays stale. The totalSupply for fee calculation drops (fewer circulating tokens), causing subsequent addFees to increment cumulativeFeePerToken by more per ETH. When the user deposits back, their stale offset captures the inflated fee rate, claiming fees for a period they held zero internal tokens.
threats.investigation_rationale: The withdraw/deposit cycle creates an asymmetry: the user's fee offset doesn't reflect their actual holding period, allowing them to capture fees distributed while they had no internal balance.

proof_of_signal.pattern: withdraw calls _transfer to move tokens to address(this) but never calls feeRedistributor.onBalanceChange

preconditions: User holds internal tokens and has a recent fee offset
Active trading generating holder fees during the withdrawal period
ERC20 token deployed for the subject (or auto-deploys on withdraw)

initial_economic_evidence.suspicious_calculation: After withdraw: totalSupply drops → addFees increments cumFPT by more per ETH. After deposit: owed = (inflated_cumFPT - stale_offset) * full_balance
initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: 1) Withdraw all tokens. 2) Wait for trades/fees. 3) Deposit back. 4) Claim inflated fees.
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: Capture fees from period of zero internal holding; concentrated fee rate benefits the attacker at expense of remaining holders

economic_questions: How much extra fee can be extracted per withdraw/deposit cycle?
Does reducing totalSupply significantly amplify per-token fee rate?
Can this be repeated indefinitely?
validation_requirements: Simulate withdraw → wait for fee distribution → deposit → claim sequence
Compare claimed amount vs fair share
Test with varying proportions of wrapped vs unwrapped tokens

preliminary_attack_economics.estimated_capital_needed: Initial token purchase + gas for withdraw/deposit cycle
preliminary_attack_economics.potential_profit_range: Proportional to fee distributions during withdrawal period
preliminary_attack_economics.estimated_profit_margin: Higher when attacker holds large proportion of supply
preliminary_attack_economics.attack_duration: Multi-block: withdraw, wait for trades, deposit, claim

market_conditions_analysis.liquidity_requirements: Must hold significant portion of subject's tokens
market_conditions_analysis.market_impact_assessment: Remaining holders receive lower per-token fees

potential_victim_impact.direct_victims: Other internal token holders for the same subject
potential_victim_impact.estimated_impact_per_user: Reduced fee earnings proportional to attacker's manipulation
potential_victim_impact.total_value_at_risk: Holder fees during each withdrawal period

investigation_priorities.parameter_analysis: Typical fee volumes and token distributions
investigation_priorities.calculation_validation: Compute exact fee capture vs fair share

threats.title: onBalanceChange Bug Destroys Buyer's Accumulated Fees on Every Normal Buy
threats.hypothesis_description: During every buy transaction, _transferFees calls onBalanceChange(subject, buyer) which sets the buyer's userFeeOffset to cumulativeFeePerToken WITHOUT first calling updateFeeCredit. Any fees the buyer had accumulated since their last offset update are permanently destroyed. This happens during normal protocol operation, not just during attacks.
threats.investigation_rationale: Every single buy transaction causes the buyer to lose their accumulated but unclaimed holder fees. This is a systematic loss affecting all users who hold tokens and continue buying more.

proof_of_signal.pattern: onBalanceChange directly sets userFeeOffset without calling updateFeeCredit to snapshot pending owed fees into unclaimedFees

preconditions: User holds tokens and has accumulated unclaimed fees (cumFPT > offset)
User buys more tokens for the same subject
holdersFeePercent > 0

initial_economic_evidence.suspicious_calculation: Before buy: owed = (cumFPT - oldOffset) * balance. After onBalanceChange: offset = cumFPT → owed = 0 → lost fees
initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: No attacker needed — this happens automatically on every buy
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: Destroyed fees remain in FeeSplitter, benefiting other holders or becoming unclaimable dust

economic_questions: How much total value is lost across all users from this bug during normal usage?
Can savvy users exploit this by claiming before every buy (transferring value from less-aware users)?
validation_requirements: Check claimable fees before and after a buy — confirm reduction
Compare onBalanceChange implementation to correct updateFeeCredit pattern

market_conditions_analysis.market_impact_assessment: Systematic fee loss reduces holder incentive

potential_victim_impact.direct_victims: Every user who buys more tokens while holding unclaimed fees
potential_victim_impact.estimated_impact_per_user: 100% of accumulated unclaimed fees lost per buy
potential_victim_impact.total_value_at_risk: Cumulative holder fees across all users

threats.title: Protocol Fee on Sell Transactions Permanently Locked in Curves Contract
threats.hypothesis_description: In _transferFees for sells, sellValue = price - protocolFee - subjectFee - referralFee - holderFee is sent to the seller. The protocolFee is deducted from the seller's payout but is never sent to protocolFeeDestination. On buys, protocolFee goes to protocolFeeDestination; on sells, it stays locked in the Curves contract with no withdrawal mechanism.
threats.investigation_rationale: This is an asymmetric fee accounting bug. The protocol loses revenue from every sell, and the ETH becomes permanently inaccessible. Over time, significant ETH accumulates with no recovery path.

proof_of_signal.pattern: firstDestination for sell = msg.sender with sellValue = price - all fees; protocolFee never sent to protocolFeeDestination on sell path

preconditions: protocolFeePercent > 0
Any sell transaction occurs

initial_economic_evidence.suspicious_calculation: On sell: firstDestination.call{value: sellValue} sends to msg.sender. protocolFee is subtracted from sellValue but never routed anywhere.
initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: Not an attack vector per se but systematic value leak: every sell locks protocolFee ETH in contract
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: Protocol loses protocolFeePercent of every sell's price. Over time, contract accumulates irrecoverable ETH.

economic_questions: What proportion of total volume is sells?
How much ETH accumulates over protocol lifetime?
Is there any withdrawal mechanism that could recover it?
validation_requirements: Track Curves contract balance before/after sell — confirm protocolFee stays
Verify no withdrawal/sweep function exists

potential_victim_impact.estimated_impact_per_user: Sellers lose protocolFee from payout without it reaching protocol

investigation_priorities.calculation_validation: Trace ETH flow in _transferFees for isBuy=false

threats.title: Referral Fee on Sells Permanently Lost When No Referral Destination Set
threats.hypothesis_description: In _transferFees for sells, the referralFee is deducted from the seller's payout (sellValue = price - protocolFee - subjectFee - referralFee - holderFee). When referralFeeDestination is address(0), the referral .call is skipped via (true, bytes('')), but the ETH remains in the contract. On buys without referral, the protocol gets the referral fee. On sells without referral, it's permanently locked.
threats.investigation_rationale: This creates an asymmetric fee handling between buys and sells. Sellers systematically lose the referral fee portion when no referral is set.

proof_of_signal.pattern: On sell with no referral: referralFee deducted from sellValue but (true, bytes('')) skips sending it anywhere

preconditions: referralFeePercent > 0
referralFeeDestination[curvesTokenSubject] == address(0)
Sell transaction occurs

initial_economic_evidence.suspicious_calculation: sellValue deducts referralFee; referral .call uses (true, bytes('')) when undefined — ETH stays in contract
initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: Systematic value leak on every sell for subjects without referral

economic_questions: What proportion of subjects have referral destinations set?
What is the referralFeePercent × sell volume over time?
validation_requirements: Execute sell without referral, verify ETH stays in contract
Compare buy path (protocol gets referral fee) vs sell path (fee stuck)

potential_victim_impact.direct_victims: All sellers on curves without referral destinations
potential_victim_impact.total_value_at_risk: referralFeePercent * total sell volume for non-referred subjects

investigation_priorities.calculation_validation: Trace sell flow with no referral set

threats.title: Excess ETH From Buy Overpayment Permanently Locked in Curves Contract
threats.hypothesis_description: _buyCurvesToken checks msg.value >= price + totalFee but never refunds the difference. Any excess ETH sent by the buyer (from gas estimation errors, UI rounding, or intentional overpayment) is permanently trapped in the Curves contract with no withdrawal mechanism.
threats.investigation_rationale: While individual overpayments may be small, smart contract callers and aggregators that estimate gas conservatively could lose significant amounts over time. There is no sweep or refund function.

proof_of_signal.pattern: if (msg.value < price + totalFee) revert InsufficientPayment(); — only checks minimum, no refund of excess

preconditions: User sends more ETH than exact price + totalFee
No withdrawal mechanism exists in the contract

initial_economic_evidence.suspicious_calculation: msg.value - (price + totalFee) = excess ETH that stays in contract
initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: Not an attack — systematic user loss from overpayment
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: Cumulative locked ETH grows over protocol lifetime

economic_questions: How likely are overpayments in practice (UI behavior, contract integrations)?
Is there any mechanism to retrieve locked ETH?
validation_requirements: Send excess ETH with buy, verify contract balance increases by excess
Confirm no admin withdrawal function exists

investigation_priorities.calculation_validation: Verify no refund mechanism in any code path

threats.title: getPrice Arithmetic Underflow Prevents Multi-Token Initial Purchase
threats.hypothesis_description: getPrice(supply=0, amount>1) causes an arithmetic underflow revert. The sum2 calculation evaluates (supply - 1 + amount) which computes supply - 1 first. In Solidity 0.8.7 with checked arithmetic, 0 - 1 reverts. This forces subjects to buy exactly 1 token initially, preventing batch bootstrapping. The subject must then buy additional tokens in a separate transaction at a higher price.
threats.investigation_rationale: This forces a two-step curve initialization: first buy 1 token, then buy more in subsequent transactions. On chains where transactions could be observed between blocks, this exposes the subject to price competition on the second buy.

proof_of_signal.pattern: sum2 uses (supply - 1 + amount) — evaluates supply - 1 first, underflows when supply = 0 in Solidity 0.8.7

preconditions: supply == 0 (new curve being created)
amount > 1 (subject wants to buy multiple tokens)

initial_economic_evidence.suspicious_calculation: (supply - 1 + amount) where supply=0 → (0 - 1) underflows in checked arithmetic before adding amount
initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: Subjects forced into 2-step initialization; second buy at higher price
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: Subject pays more for initial tokens (price at supply=1 vs supply=0 for batch)

economic_questions: What is the price difference between buying N tokens at supply=0 atomically vs. 1 at supply=0 then N-1 at supply=1?
Can other users buy between the subject's two transactions on Form Network?
validation_requirements: Call getPrice(0, 2) — confirm revert
Call getPrice(0, 1) — confirm success (returns 0)

market_conditions_analysis.market_impact_assessment: Subjects pay slightly more for initial tokens

potential_victim_impact.total_value_at_risk: Minimal per instance, cumulative across all curves

investigation_priorities.calculation_validation: Confirm underflow in Solidity 0.8.7 checked mode
